title: Structural constraint on delayed-choice quantum eraser architectures
abstract: Delayed-choice quantum eraser (DCQE) experiments are often presented as challenging classical causal intuitions by correlating detection events with choices implemented at later times. While it is well understood that post-selection plays a crucial role in producing the observed interference patterns, the structural features underlying such correlations are rarely analyzed within a unified framework. In this work, we introduce a simple probabilistic constraint applicable to idealized DCQE architectures. We show that four intuitive properties -- statistical independence of the choice, absence of losses, deterministic routing conditioned on the choice, and distinct conditional detection distributions -- cannot be simultaneously satisfied. This incompatibility provides a transparent classification of DCQE schemes and clarifies how conditional interference patterns arise without invoking exotic mechanisms.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the seemingly paradoxical correlations in delayed-choice quantum eraser experiments are actually a result of incompatible structural assumptions. It proves that any realistic implementation must necessarily involve losses, non-deterministic routing, or coarse-graining to remain consistent with the independence of the measurement choice.
main_problem: The paper addresses the apparent paradox in Delayed-Choice Quantum Eraser (DCQE) experiments, where measurement choices at a later time seem to correlate with earlier detection events.
main_result: The author proves a no-go theorem stating that four intuitive properties of idealized DCQE architectures—independence of choice, absence of losses, deterministic routing, and distinct conditional distributions—are mutually incompatible.
method: A purely probabilistic analysis using elementary probability constraints and a framework of minimal compatibility relations between random variables, independent of specific quantum dynamics.
model_or_system: A generic bipartite DCQE scenario involving a signal system (S) and an idler system (I), modeled via random variables for detection outcomes, experimental choices, and density operators.
key_observables: Detection positions (X), experimental settings (C), coincidence counts, and conditional detection distributions P(X|D).
important_parameters: Loss rate (p), probability of erasure choice (q), and the minimum loss rate threshold p_min = q^2/2.
main_assumptions: The core derivation relies on the assumption of statistical independence between the choice and the signal detection (X is independent of C).
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates Architecture I (coarse-graining leading to interference cancellation); Figure 2 illustrates Architecture II (non-deterministic routing in a Mach-Zehnder setup).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the DCQE paradox, defines a probabilistic model for the system, presents a no-go theorem for idealized architectures, classifies DCQE schemes into three types based on which property they violate, and provides an analytical derivation of loss rate constraints.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental structural explanation for the 'paradoxical' nature of DCQE, showing that losses are not just experimental flaws but a structural necessity to maintain causality and independence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
